text:
Problem Overview

In this problem, students analyze various methods for making fair decisions. Fictional students propose ways to make a number of decisions, which students need to evaluate. The proposals suggest some new ways to use spinners, number cubes, blocks, slips of paper, and drawing straws to help students think about probability simulations.

Launch (Getting Started)

Connecting to Prior Knowledge

In this problem, students use all of the methods or tools they have learned to evaluate the fairness of an activity. Students must use what they have already learned to decide if their chosen strategy is fair and how to use that strategy to create a fair event.

Suggested Questions

  • What does it mean to choose fairly? (Everyone has an equally likely chance of being chosen. The choosing is done randomly.)

Presenting the Challenge

Explain the situation in the introduction on choosing a messenger to send to the office. Ask students if they have any suggestions to fairly select a student. Have students work in groups of 3-4 For the Initial Challenge, you might want to divide up the possible tools to investigate and have each group analyze two to three tools to save time. Have available blank spinners (Learning Aid 3.1: Blank Spinner), number cubes, coins, colored cubes, slips of paper, straws, and bags for students who want to test their ideas.

Implementation Note: You might want to have a brief conversation after the Initial Challenge before having groups work on the What If . . . ? Situations.
